Joshua 11 confronts us with the reality of divine judgment in its most difficult form. The northern coalition against Israel was vast—a multitude like sand on the seashore with horses and chariots—yet God's word to Joshua stripped away all fear: "Do not be afraid of them, for by this time tomorrow I will deliver all of them slain before Israel." This is not a promise that victory will be easy or that the cost will be small. It is a promise that the outcome is already decided. God does not ask Joshua to believe in his own strength or in Israel's military superiority. He asks him to trust that what the Lord has spoken will come to pass. Joshua obeys without hesitation, attacking suddenly at the waters of Merom, and the Lord delivers them into Israel's hands. The entire northern resistance is destroyed—no survivors remain. This is the fulfillment of what God promised when Israel first entered Canaan.

Yet verses 19-20 present us with something we cannot minimize or explain away. The text explicitly states that the Lord hardened the hearts of these kings so that they would not make peace with Israel but instead engage in battle, and specifically so that they would be annihilated without mercy. This is not incidental detail. It is the theological explanation for why every nation chose war over surrender. God's purposes included not just the possession of the land but the judgment of those whose sin had ripened for destruction. The Canaanite nations had centuries to turn from their idolatry and wickedness; instead, they had sunk deeper into abomination. Their destruction was just, and the hardening of their hearts was God's final judgment upon them. We may struggle with this—and we should not pretend the struggle is simple—but we must not soften what Scripture says or imagine that God acts without justice.

The repetition throughout this chapter of what Joshua did "as the LORD had commanded Moses" anchors everything in God's explicit covenant promises made generations before. This was not Joshua's innovation or Israel's cruelty; it was obedience to what God had commanded. The complete destruction, the hamstringing of horses, the burning of Hazor—all of it traced back to the word of the Lord through Moses. Verses 15 and 20 especially emphasize this connection: Joshua did exactly what he was commanded, leaving nothing undone. When the conquest finally ends, the land rests from war. Israel possesses what God had sworn to give them. But that rest came only through obedience to God's word, however costly, however foreign to modern sensibilities. What this chapter demands of us is not agreement with every detail of ancient warfare, but submission to the reality that God judges nations, that His purposes cannot be thwarted, and that true obedience sometimes requires us to trust Him beyond what we naturally understand.
